name: B.S. in Architecture and M.S. in Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The undergraduate B.S. architecture base is coherent through the mid-program (studios ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296 -> 395, Construction I/II, History I/II), but like the standalone B.S. the upper years dissolve into 'ARCH Elective'/'Free Elective' rows, and the graduate M.S. Management block is not shown. The combined-degree progression therefore cannot be read end to end.
dimensions.suggestion: Present the integrated B.S.-plus-M.S. timeline and give the upper-year electives declared categories so the path into the management year is explicit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The architecture prerequisite pool is sensible (deepestPrereqChain 6), but this program carries the largest dangling set of the group: 30 references (missingCoursesSample includes ACCT 615, ARCH 647/650/651/652/675, CE 200A/501/615/631/642/702/711, ENE 662, FIN 516). These are graduate management and civil-engineering courses outside the undergraduate catalog, so the high count is a combined-degree data caveat, but the CE-heavy sample looks mismatched for an M.S. in Management and needs verification.
dimensions.suggestion: Cross-link the graduate references to the graduate catalog, verify why civil-engineering (CE 5xx-7xx) courses appear in a management combined degree, and resolve GER placeholders.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data; only the plan and course descriptions are present. For a design-plus-management dual credential this is a real gap because the differentiator is the fusion of design and management competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish combined learning outcomes tying the B.S. architecture competencies to the M.S. Management competencies (organizational, financial, operational).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The metrics show summedTermCredits 120 against statedTotalCredits 31, a creditReconcileDelta of +89.0. The stated 31 evidently captures only the graduate M.S. Management overlay, so the large delta is a combined-degree data-consistency caveat rather than a defect, but the page neither reconciles nor shows the full combined total.
dimensions.suggestion: State the full combined total and the B.S.-vs-M.S. credit split so the 31-credit overlay is contextualized and the plan reconciles.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The eight-term B.S. plan is legible with labeled terms and per-term credits, but it leans on many placeholder elective rows, the graduate management year is not depicted, and 30 dangling references (including seemingly mismatched CE courses) make the overall page read as unresolved.
dimensions.suggestion: Depict the graduate management terms, label each elective slot with its category/credit rule, and resolve or verify the dangling CE/management references.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Pairing a B.S. in Architecture with an M.S. in Management is relevant to design-business and firm-operations careers, and this program's sampled electives are notably current: DD 320 Robotics for Architects and Designers, DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments, ARCH 571 Sustainable City, and CE 200 Surveying show emerging-technology and sustainability exposure. The main gap is that business/economics bridge content is not visible in the undergraduate portion.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required construction-economics/business-of-design course in the undergraduate years and foreground DD 320/321 as design-technology bridges into the management curriculum.

descriptionQuality.justification: Several sampled descriptions are strong and current (DD 320 Robotics on Arduino/sensors/actuators, DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments, ARCH 571 Sustainable City, CE 200 Surveying with GPS/mapping content), but continuation studios (ARCH 196/296) are thin/boilerplate, AD 325 Entrepreneurship has an empty description, and ARCH 583 'ST:' is a stub.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Clarity is materially reduced by 30 dangling references to graduate management and civil-engineering courses outside the undergraduate catalog, compounded by unparenthesized compound architecture prereqs (ARCH 395, ARCH 211) and multi-condition strings in DD 320/321. The dangling count is largely a combined-degree caveat, but the mismatched CE references make the prerequisite picture hard to trust.

missingTopics.topic: Construction economics and business of design
missingTopics.rationale: The undergraduate plan lacks estimating, pro-formas, or design-firm economics, exactly the bridge a management combined degree should own before the graduate business block.
AD 325 Entrepreneurship for Designers exists but has no description and is not clearly required; a design-plus-management degree should make entrepreneurship a documented core.

missingTopics.topic: Required building-technology core (structures and environmental systems)
missingTopics.rationale: As in the standalone B.S., Structures and ECS are not required here, so graduates can bypass core technical competencies before entering management study.

missingTopics.topic: Project and operations management for design practice
missingTopics.rationale: No undergraduate course addresses scheduling, staffing, or operations, the practical management skills that connect the two degrees.

missingTopics.topic: Data analytics for design and operations
missingTopics.rationale: With DD 320/321 signaling technology interest, the plan would benefit from a data/analytics course supporting management decision-making.

emergingTrends.rationale: DD 320 Robotics for Architects and DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments already position the program at the design-technology frontier; formalizing them as a track would strengthen the management-of-technology angle.
AD 325 Entrepreneurship plus the management degree can prepare graduates to launch and run design-technology ventures, a growing career path.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and computational design tooling
emergingTrends.rationale: AI-driven design and estimating are reshaping design-business practice; the studio and DD sequence are natural places to teach responsible AI adoption, replacing the aging CS 104 emphasis.

emergingTrends.trend: Sustainability economics and ESG in the built environment
emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 571 Sustainable City provides a base to connect sustainability with the management curriculum's financial and ESG decision-making.

emergingTrends.trend: Smart, sensor-driven buildings and IoT operations
emergingTrends.rationale: DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments and CE 200 Surveying point toward sensor/data-rich buildings whose operation is increasingly a management discipline.

topSuggestions: State the full combined B.S. + M.S. Management credit total and split so the +89 reconciliation gap (31 stated vs. 120 summed) is contextualized and the plan reconciles.
Verify the dangling references: 30 references include civil-engineering (CE 5xx-7xx) courses that appear mismatched for an M.S. in Management and should be corrected or explained.
Publish combined learning outcomes fusing B.S. architecture and M.S. Management competencies.

Add construction-economics/business-of-design content and make AD 325 Entrepreneurship and the DD 320/321 technology electives explicit bridges into the management block; introduce a required building-technology core.
Depict the graduate management terms, resolve GER placeholders, and clean thin/empty descriptions (AD 325, ARCH 583 'ST:').
